coredata.dc:title: Attaining a wide photoluminescence Stokes-shift of carbon dots obtained from waste biomass
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Rasheidi, Masoud
Alabdulhadi, Arwa
Khan, Firoz
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the tremendous physicochemical and quantum confinement characteristics of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), they have revealed exciting and essential visions in the field of energy conversion and energy storage. However, due to their low photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and/or short Stokes-shift, limits their application in photovoltaic (PV). In this regard, a novel and cost-effective method was used to synthesize the CQDs using waste biomass. Furthermore, a comprehensive study was made to explore the applicability of the CQDs as photon downconverters/downshifters in PVs. The average size of the CQDs are 5.1, 4.4, and 3.9 nm for the synthesis temperature of 160, 180, and 200 ℃, respectively. The EDS analysis revealed that C, O, Mg, Na, Cl, and K are present in all the CQDs. The PL emission peak position is blue-shifted with the rise in the synthesis temperature. The Stokes-shift is reduced with a rise in the synthesis temperature. The highest Stokes-shift was obtained at excitation wavelength of 300 nm for CQDs synthesized at 160 ℃. The PLQY increased by 3.5 and 7.2-folds for the rise in the temperature from 160 to 180 and 200 ℃, respectively. The investigation revealed that the applicability of CQDs can be enriched via the selection of the synthesis parameters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste food materials
Carbon quantum dots
Luminescence
Photoluminescence quantum yield
Stokes-shift

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM images of CQDs synthesized at (a, b) 160 ℃, (c, d) 180 ℃, and (e, f) 200 ℃.
Size distribution and Gaussian fitting of CQD samples (a) 160 ℃, (b) 180 ℃, and (c) 200 ℃.
(a) Scanning electron microscopy image and (b–h) energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping of CQDs synthesized at 180 ℃.
Deconvoluted absorption spectrum of CQDs synthesized at (a) 160 ℃, (b) 180 ℃, and (c) 200 ℃. (d) Deconvoluted absorption peak position and (e) individual absorption peak area of CQDs.
Absorption spectra of carbon quantum dots synthesized at (a) 160 ℃, (b) 180 ℃, and (c) 200 ℃. (d) Deconvoluted absorption peak position and (e) absorption area.
Optical images of CQDs (left bottom) in white light and (right bottom) in UV light of different CQDs. The cyan-green color of the CQDs revealed the emission of wavelength (top).

Photoluminescence (a) excitation spectra for CQDs (160C, 180C, and 200C) and emission spectra of CQDs synthesized at (b) 160 ℃, (c) 180 ℃, and (d) 200 ℃ for different excitation wavelengths in the range of 300–480 nm.
Comparison of the photoluminescence (a) peak position and (b) peak intensity of CQDs for the excitation wavelength of 440, 460, and 480 nm.

Deconvoluted photoluminescence emission spectrum of CQDs synthesized at (a) 160 ℃, (b) 180 ℃, and (c) 200 ℃. (d) Comparison of three dominating peaks (peak-1, peak-2, and peak-3) (e) positions of emission peaks of CQDs.
3D map of the photoluminescence (a) peak position, (b) Stokes-shift, and (c) peak intensity of CQDs synthesized at various temperatures.
Obtained photoluminescence peak position and Stokes-shift at various synthesis temperatures.

head.ce:title: Attaining a wide photoluminescence Stokes-shift of carbon dots obtained from waste biomass

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Renewable Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science and Engineering Department, King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals (KFUPM), Dhahran 31261, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals (KFUPM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Science and Engineering Department, King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals (KFUPM), Dhahran 31261, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to the tremendous physicochemical and quantum confinement characteristics of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), they have revealed exciting and essential visions in the field of energy conversion and energy storage. However, due to their low photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and/or short Stokes-shift, limits their application in photovoltaic (PV). In this regard, a novel and cost-effective method was used to synthesize the CQDs using waste biomass. Furthermore, a comprehensive study was made to explore the applicability of the CQDs as photon downconverters/downshifters in PVs. The average size of the CQDs are 5.1, 4.4, and 3.9 nm for the synthesis temperature of 160, 180, and 200 ℃, respectively. The EDS analysis revealed that C, O, Mg, Na, Cl, and K are present in all the CQDs. The PL emission peak position is blue-shifted with the rise in the synthesis temperature. The Stokes-shift is reduced with a rise in the synthesis temperature. The highest Stokes-shift was obtained at excitation wavelength of 300 nm for CQDs synthesized at 160 ℃. The PLQY increased by 3.5 and 7.2-folds for the rise in the temperature from 160 to 180 and 200 ℃, respectively. The investigation revealed that the applicability of CQDs can be enriched via the selection of the synthesis parameters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For many decades carbon-based materials are considered potential candidates for their application in various fields, such as energy, agriculture, medicine, bio-imaging sensors engineering, etc. [1]. Though, due to the absence of a suitable bandgap, the macroscopic carbon-based materials make it problematic to work as an efficient fluorescent material. It is well known that the material's properties are altered by the reduction of their dimensions within the atomic scale. These materials are recognized as “nanomaterials (NMs)”. The nanomaterials with zero dimension (0D) are referred to as “quantum dots (QDs)”. The carbon-based QDs (CQDs) have offered a vast range of prospects for the design of plenty of materials with diverse properties for numerous uses [2,3]. Carbon-based NMs are considered cost-effective materials with remarkable mechanical strength, tunable electronic properties, and excellent electrochemical and thermal, and chemical stabilities [4]. Moreover, due to the quantum confinement effect, the CQDs with sizes less than 10 nm act as luminescent materials [5]. It attracted extensively enormous consideration towards advancement owing to their cost-effectiveness, good thermal and chemical stabilities, minute venomousness, great biocompatibility, easy surface amendments variable fluorescence (PL), and high PL quantum yield (PL QY) [6,7]. These exceptional properties empower CQDs to have potential applications in bio-imaging, biologic probes, biomedicine, catalysis, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), solar cells, and drug delivery [8–16]. Previous, the CQDs were synthesized by laser ablation of carbon (PL QY = 10 %) [17], and polymerization of citric acid and ethylenediamine (PL QY = 80 %) [18], arc discharge [6], biomass [19,20], chemical oxidation [21], ultrasonication [22], solvothermal [23], microwave [24–26] and, thermal [27,28] processes. Generally, the shape of the CQDs is spherical, often synthesized using small molecules, assembling of polymers/biomass, polymerization, crosslinking, and carbonization thru “bottom-up” approaches (namely, combustion, thermal treatment, etc.) [29–34]. Among the CQD synthesis methods, biomass-originated CQDs are most auspicious because of their cost-effectiveness, and rich availability of raw materials, environment-friendly, natural doping of heteroatoms (i.e., B, N, S, P, etc.).

Herein, the CQDs were synthesized from biomass using a hydrothermal process. In this method, a raw material of skin of sweet melon was used. In this process, green synthesis was adopted (no chemical was used). Thus, the obtained CQDs were much more suitable for bio application.

First, the fruit waste material (sweet melon skin) was dried in the Sun. The powder was formed via a mixture grinder. The powder was washed thoroughly in deionized water (DIW) followed by drying in an oven at 70 ℃. Then 7 g of the powder was dispersed in 70 ml of DIW. The dispersed mixture was transferred to a stainless steel-lined Teflon autoclave (100 ml). The hydrothermal reaction was carried out for 24 h at various reaction temperatures (160, 180, and 200 ℃). After natural cooling, CQDs containing suspension were separated from the hydrochar via a centrifuge process at 5000 rpm. The suspension was further filtered using a syringe filter of the pore size of 0.22 μm. The CQDs synthesized at 160, 180, and 200 ℃ are referred to as CQD-160, CQD-180, and CQD-200, respectively.

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR; Smart iTR NICOLET iS10) transmission spectra were investigated at room temperature over the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the CQD were obtained using a (JEOL SEM; JSM6610LV). The absorption spectra were obtained using UV–Vis spectroscopy (JASCO UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer; Model: V-670). The high-resolution morphology and size of the CQDs were obtained using transmission electron microscopy (JEOL; Model: JEM2100F). PL spectra were obtained using PL spectroscopy (FluoroLog-Modular Spectrofluorometer, Horiba Scientific).

For the bonding analysis, FTIR transmission spectra were obtained to carry out the bonding analysis of the synthesized CQDs. Fig. 1 shows the FTIR spectra of the CQDs. A broad absorption band centered at 3352 cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of O-H. Another small peak is seen at 2980 cm−1, which is associated with stretching vibrations of C-H [35]. The absorption peaks positioned at 1643, 1461, 1376, and 1137 cm−1 are attributed to C=O, N-H, C-O-C, and C-O, respectively. The broad absorption peak centered at ∼ 700 cm−1 is due to the presence of C-H bonding of the benzene ring [35].

The morphology and size of the CQDs are examined using TEM. The TEM and HRTEM images of CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 , which also confirmed the uniform CQDs growth. The average size of the CQDs are 5.1, 4.4, and 3.9 nm for the synthesis temperature of 160, 180, and 200 ℃, respectively. The particle size distribution is also determined. The particle size distributions and their Gaussian fit is shown in Fig. 3 .

The SEM images along with EDS elemental mapping are also obtained to examine the elemental distribution. The SEM image of CQD-180 is illustrated in Fig. 4a . The elemental EDS mapping revealed that the synthesized CQDs are C-rich, in which the second dominant element is O (Fig. 4b–d). The other present elements are Mg, Cl, Na and K (Fig. 4e–g). The Cl is present due to traces of the chlorine water used to wash the sweet melon. Furthermore, the EDS spectra are shown in Fig. 5a –c. The atomic percentage of each element is shown in the chart bar (Fig. 5d). The obtained content C is 57.30, 61.9, and 52.7 % in CQD-160, CQD-180, and CQD-200, respectively. The corresponding atomic percentage of O is 36.30, 25.60, and 32.80 %, respectively. However, the 3.2 at % of K is observed in CQD-160, which is gradually increased to 6.10 and 8.60 at % for the synthesis temperature of 180 and 200 ℃, respectively.

Fig. 6 shows UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CQDs dispersed in DIW. All samples have a broad absorption in the 250–400 nm wavelength range. The absorption peaks were de-convoluted into four peaks. The absorption peaks are arisen due to π → π* transition. The deep UV transition for the λ value below 250 nm is related to π → π* transition of C=C. However, the absorption at λ > 250 nm is ascribed due to the n → π* transition of C=O bonding [27]. The de-convoluted peaks obtained for CQD-160 are positioned at 295.5, 303.9, 319.2, and 339.6 nm, which correspond to 4.19, 4.08, 3.88, and 3.65 eV, respectively (Fig. 6a). The first two peaks are red-shifted for increasing the synthesis temperature to 180 ℃, while the position of other peaks remains constant (Fig. 6b). Further rise in the synthesis temperature, peak-1, peak-2, and peak-4 blue-shifted, however, peak-3 slightly red-shifted (Fig. 6c and e). A comparison of the absorption spectra is made (Fig. 6d). The highest absorption was obtained for CQD-180 (Fig. 6e). For the quantitative analysis, the absorption peak area was calculated. It was found that the integrated area peak-1, peak-3, and peak-4 increased for CQD-180, while the area of peak-2 remained unchanged. The area of peak-2 slightly increased for the synthesis temperature of 200 ℃. The area of peak-1, peak-3, and peak-4 of CQD-200 is nearly similar to that of CQD-160. The obtained total area is 34, 59, and 41 (a. u.) for CQD-160, CQD-180, and CQD-200, respectively.

The optical image of the CQDs depressed in DIW in white light (left) and in UV light (right, λex = 395 nm) is shown in Fig. 7 . It can be seen that the color of the CQDs in white light was light brown. However, after UV illumination, the CQDs are shined with cyan-green color. The cyan-green color belongs to the wavelength range of 500–540 nm. For the determination of the down-conversion/shifting performance, the PL emission spectra were obtained. The PL emission spectra was recorded for the excitation wavelength of 540 nm (Fig. 8a ). It was observed that the main excitation peak is obtained at 420 nm. Furthermore, a wide range of excitation wavelengths (λex = 300–480 nm) was used to get emission spectra (Fig. 8b–d). The PL peak position and Stokes-shift are listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that the PL peak position slightly shifted with the variation of the excitation wavelength due to the presence of defect level. The highest PL intensity was achieved for the excitation wavelength of 480 nm for all the CQDs. The obtained fluorescent can be explained on the basis of the Frank-Condon principle [35]. The Stokes-shift obtained for the excitation wavelength of 340 nm are 205.77, 190.51, 176.94 nm for the synthesis temperatures of 160, 180, and 200 ℃, respectively. At excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the Stokes-shift obtained in this work is in the range of 108–138 nm.

The PL peak area of peak-1 increased with an increase in the synthesis temperature to 180 ℃, which remained constant for higher temperatures (Fig. 10d). However, the area of peak-2 and peak-3 gradually increased with the rise in the temperature. The overall peak area is found to be 8.97×106, 3.16×107, and 6.44×107 for CQD-160, CQD-180, and CQD-200, respectively. The PL quantum yield (PLQY) is directly proportional to the PL emission peak area. Thus, it can be concluded that the PLQY of the CQDs is increased by 3.5 and 7.2-fold via increasing the synthesis temperature. However, the PL peak position blue-shifted with increasing synthesis temperature (Fig. 10e). The corresponding obtained Stokes-shift is 167.2, 152.7, and 135.1 nm for the CQD-160, CQD-180, and CQD-200, respectively. It shows that Stokes-shift gradually shrunk with a rise in synthesis temperature. For the solar cell application, the PLQY, and Stokes-shift are key factors to maximize the performance. The Stokes-shift obtained here is much greater than the Stokes-shift reported for graphene quantum dots [27,28] and CQDs [20,37]. For the detailed analysis, 3D graphs were plotted, in which two parameters (synthesis temperature and excitation wavelength) are kept along X- and Y-axes, however, PL peak position, Stokes-shift, and PL intensity are taken along to Z-axis (Fig. 11a – c).

It can be concluded that the PL emission peak position and Stokes-shift are the highest for the synthesis temperature of 160 ℃ and the excitation wavelength of 460 nm. However, the PL peak intensity is maximum for the synthesis temperature of 200 ℃ and the excitation wavelength of 460 nm.

The waste food material (skin of sweet melon) used as raw materials to synthesize the carbon quantum dots using a hydrothermal process at the synthesis temperature of 160, 180, and 200 ℃. The size of the CQDs is reduced with the rise in the synthesis temperature. The average size of the CQDs are 5.1, 4.4, and 3.9 nm for the synthesis temperature of 160, 180, and 200 ℃, respectively. The EDS analysis revealed that C, O, Mg, Na, Cl, and K are present in all the CQDs. The CQDs are O-rich, the atomic percentage of O is 36.30, 25.60, and 32.80 % in CQD-160, CQD-180, and CQD-200, respectively. The deconvolution of the absorption spectra suggests that four key transitions are responsible for the wide absorption. Thus, the PL emission peak position is blue-shifted with the rise in temperature. The Stokes-shift is also increased with a reduction in the synthesis temperature. However, the integrated PL peak area is enhanced with increasing temperature. The peak area increased by 3.5 and 7.2 folds for the rise in the temperature from 160 to 180 and 200 ℃, respectively. This result revealed that the PLQY can be enhanced by 3.5 and 7.2 times for increasing the temperature to 180 and 200 ℃, respectively. The obtain results suggested that the obtained CQDs offer an excellent PL property. Thus, these CQDs have potential application in catalytical activity, bioimaging, drug delivery, etc.

Masoud Al-Rasheidi: Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Arwa Alabdulhadi: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Firoz Khan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the funding (Project No. INSE2407) from the Interdisciplinary Research Center for Sustainable Energy Systems (IRC-SES), King Fahd University of Petroleum & Minerals (KFUPM), Saudi Arabia.
